name: Liancourt Rocks (Dokdo / Takeshima / 독도 / 竹島)
summary: The Liancourt Rocks (English Liancourt Rocks after the French whaler Liancourt that recorded the rocks in 1849 / Korean Dokdo 독도 'Solitary Island' / Japanese Takeshima 竹島 'Bamboo Island') are a Sea of Japan / East Sea disputed islet group comprising 91 islets and reefs (two main islets: Dongdo/Mendo 'Eastern/Female Island' and Seodo/Odo 'Western/Male Island') covering ~0.187 km² of land area within a much larger maritime EEZ context — structurally distinctive globally as the only major bilateral territorial dispute between two democratic allies of the same superpower (United States) where the superpower has explicitly declined to take a position (US has consistently maintained neutrality between Japan and South Korea claims), and as the only dispute where the administering state (South Korea, since 1954) has built civilian residential infrastructure including a permanent two-person civilian residence (the Kim Sung-do family lived on Dokdo from 1968-2018; Kim Shin-yeol since 2018) explicitly to entrench sovereignty. The Liancourt Rocks are administered by South Korea since the 1954 establishment of a permanent Korean Coast Guard presence, as part of Ulleung-eup of Ulleung County, North Gyeongsang Province (since 2000 administrative consolidation). Japan claims the Liancourt Rocks as part of Okinoshima Town, Oki District, Shimane Prefecture (since 1905 Japanese Cabinet decision). South Korea formally rejected Japanese sovereignty via the 1952 Syngman Rhee Line declaration. The Liancourt Rocks are not claimed by any third state — making them a purely bilateral Korea-Japan dispute distinct from the multilateral Spratly/Paracel/Senkaku disputes. South Korea's family-law framework operates under the Korean Civil Code (민법 1958) applied via Ulleung County administrative framework. Parental authority and child custody operate under Korean Civil Code Articles 909-927 (친권 / chinkwon / parental authority). The Daegu District Court is the first-instance court for Ulleung County including the Liancourt Rocks; the Daegu High Court is the intermediate appellate court; final appellate jurisdiction lies with the Supreme Court of South Korea. The Liancourt Rocks are silent on 'parental alienation' as a statutory label. South Korea is a Hague Convention 1980 party (acceded 13 December 2012, effective 1 March 2013) — Liancourt Rocks Hague applicability via South Korean territorial extension is unrecognised by Japan.

statutory_framework.title: Syngman Rhee Line Declaration
statutory_framework.relevance: South Korean President Syngman Rhee Presidential Proclamation of 18 January 1952 establishing 'Peace Line' (Syngman Rhee Line) including Liancourt Rocks within South Korean jurisdiction.

statutory_framework.citation: Korean Civil Code 1958 (applicable in Liancourt Rocks per Korean administration)
statutory_framework.title: Korean Civil Code
statutory_framework.relevance: Korean Civil Code Articles 909-927 governing 친권 (chinkwon / parental authority) applicable in Liancourt Rocks via Ulleung County administrative framework.

statutory_framework.title: Japanese Cabinet Decision 1905
statutory_framework.relevance: Japanese Cabinet Decision of 28 January 1905 incorporating Liancourt Rocks (as Takeshima) into Okinoshima, Oki District, Shimane Prefecture — Japanese position: terra nullius incorporation; Korean position: incorporation contemporaneous with Japanese protectorate over Korea (1905 Eulsa Treaty).

apex_courts.name: Supreme Court of South Korea (대법원)
apex_courts.role: Final appellate court for civil and criminal matters from Liancourt Rocks via South Korean administration.

apex_courts.name: Daegu High Court
apex_courts.role: Intermediate appellate court for civil and criminal matters from North Gyeongsang Province including Liancourt Rocks.

professional_regulators.name: Korean Ministry of Health and Welfare
professional_regulators.role: Korean professional regulatory framework applicable in Liancourt Rocks via South Korean administration.

anonymisation_convention: Liancourt Rocks-related decisions are anonymised per Korean court practice using initials.

key_developments.title: Liancourt naming by French whaler
key_developments.description: French whaler Liancourt recorded the rocks in January 1849 — Western naming origin. Pre-modern documentation establishing 19th-century European cartographic recognition.

key_developments.title: Korean Empire Edict 41 of 25 October 1900
key_developments.description: Korean Empire Imperial Edict No. 41 of 25 October 1900 establishing Uldo County including 'Seokdo' (석도 / Stone Island) — Korean position: 'Seokdo' = modern Dokdo; Japanese position: 'Seokdo' = different feature. Pivotal Korean position foundation for pre-1905 sovereignty claim.

key_developments.title: Japanese Cabinet incorporation of Takeshima
key_developments.description: Japanese Cabinet Decision of 28 January 1905 incorporating Liancourt Rocks (as Takeshima) into Okinoshima, Oki District, Shimane Prefecture — Japanese position: terra nullius incorporation; Korean position: incorporation contemporaneous with Japanese protectorate over Korea (1905 Eulsa Treaty 17 November 1905 establishing Japanese Protectorate over Korea), invalidating incorporation as colonial expansionism.

key_developments.title: Japanese surrender + SCAPIN 677 administrative separation
key_developments.description: Japanese surrender 2 September 1945 ending WWII — SCAPIN 677 of 29 January 1946 (Supreme Commander Allied Powers Instruction Note) administratively separated Liancourt Rocks from Japan + placed under Allied Military Government of Korea framework. Korean position: SCAPIN 677 transferred sovereignty; Japanese position: SCAPIN 677 was provisional administrative measure only.

key_developments.title: San Francisco Peace Treaty + Rusk Letter
key_developments.description: San Francisco Peace Treaty of 8 September 1951 — Japan renounced sovereignty over Korea but Liancourt Rocks not specifically mentioned; Rusk Letter of 9 August 1951 from US State Department Assistant Secretary Dean Rusk to South Korean Ambassador stated US position that Dokdo had been Japanese territory since 1905 and not part of Korea — Japanese position relies on Rusk Letter; Korean position: Rusk Letter not binding bilateral framework + did not represent unified Allied position.

key_developments.title: Syngman Rhee Line Declaration
key_developments.description: South Korean President Syngman Rhee Presidential Proclamation of 18 January 1952 establishing 'Peace Line' (Syngman Rhee Line) including Liancourt Rocks within South Korean jurisdiction — pivotal modern South Korean claim assertion + foundation for subsequent Korean de-facto administration framework.

key_developments.title: Korean permanent Coast Guard presence
key_developments.description: South Korean Coast Guard established permanent presence on Liancourt Rocks in 1954 — foundation of South Korean de-facto administration + structurally distinctive 70+ year continuous Korean Coast Guard presence framework.

key_developments.title: Kim Sung-do family Dokdo residence
key_developments.description: Kim Sung-do and wife Choi Jong-deok established permanent civilian residence on Dokdo in 1968 — sovereignty-entrenchment civilian-presence framework — Kim Sung-do died 2018 + succeeded by daughter Kim Shin-yeol — structurally distinctive globally only dispute where administering state has built civilian residential infrastructure to entrench sovereignty.

key_developments.title: Lee Myung-bak Dokdo visit + Korean Hague accession
key_developments.description: South Korean President Lee Myung-bak visited Dokdo on 10 August 2012 — first South Korean Presidential visit to disputed islands, triggering significant Japan-Korea diplomatic tension. South Korea acceded to Hague Convention 1980 effective 13 December 2012 (effective 1 March 2013) — Korean Hague applicability to Liancourt Rocks via Korean territorial extension unrecognised by Japan.

key_developments.title: Yoon-Kishida-Camp-David-trilateral + improving-Japan-Korea-relations + Liancourt-Rocks-dispute-de-emphasis framework
key_developments.description: Yoon Suk-yeol Korean Presidency + Fumio Kishida Japanese Premiership + Camp David Trilateral Summit August 2023 with US President Biden substantively distinctive improving-Japan-Korea-relations framework with Yoon Suk-yeol explicitly declining Dokdo visits to avoid Japan-Korea tension + continuing Korean Coast Guard presence + Kim Shin-yeol residence framework substantively significant institutional context. Korean Ministry of Justice institutional positions addressing PA-related issues within Korean Civil Code Articles 909-927 framework applied via Daegu District Court. Substantively significant Sea-of-Japan/East-Sea bilateral disputed-islet-group institutional jurisprudential development.

key_developments.title: Continuing Korean administration + Japan continuing claim framework
key_developments.description: Continuing Korean de-facto administration 2024 — 70 years of Korean Coast Guard presence since 1954 + continuing Kim Shin-yeol residence since 2018 + Yoon Suk-yeol declined Dokdo visit during term (improving Japan-Korea relations framework) + continuing Japanese MOFA annual Takeshima Day February 22 (Shimane Prefecture) + continuing US neutrality framework + structurally distinctive globally only major bilateral territorial dispute between two democratic allies of same superpower where superpower explicitly declines to take position.

structural_findings: Liancourt Rocks operate a civil-law Korean Civil Code framework via South Korean administration since 1954 — places Liancourt Rocks in the Sea of Japan / East Sea Korean-administered-disputed-territory cluster.

Only major bilateral territorial dispute between two democratic allies of the same superpower (United States) where the superpower has explicitly declined to take a position is structurally distinctive globally — US neutrality between Japan and South Korea claims.

Only dispute where administering state has built civilian residential infrastructure to entrench sovereignty (Kim Sung-do family 1968-2018, Kim Shin-yeol since 2018) is structurally distinctive globally — sovereignty-entrenchment civilian-residence framework.
Purely bilateral Korea-Japan dispute (no third-state claim) is structurally distinctive within Asian maritime-disputes cluster — distinct from multilateral Spratly/Paracel/Senkaku disputes.
South Korean Presidential visits (Lee Myung-bak 2012, Moon Jae-in 2017, Yoon Suk-yeol declined) are structurally distinctive within disputed-territory cluster.
Korean Hague Convention 1980 accession (2012, effective 2013) applicability unrecognised by Japan is structurally distinctive within Hague-applicability cluster.

sources.title: Japanese Ministry of Foreign Affairs Takeshima

editorial_notes: Liancourt Rocks (Dokdo / Takeshima) jurisdiction sidecar v1.1 — civil-law South-Korean-administered Sea of Japan / East Sea bilateral disputed islet group (1849 Liancourt naming + 1900 Korean Empire Edict 41 Seokdo + 1905 Japanese Cabinet Takeshima incorporation contemporaneous with Eulsa Treaty + 1945-46 SCAPIN 677 administrative separation + 1951 San Francisco Peace Treaty + Rusk Letter + 1952 Syngman Rhee Line + 1954 Korean Coast Guard permanent presence + 1968 Kim Sung-do family civilian residence + 2012 Lee Myung-bak Dokdo visit + Korean Hague accession + 2024 continuing Korean administration with Yoon Suk-yeol declined visit). Only major bilateral territorial dispute between two democratic allies of same superpower where superpower has explicitly declined to take a position globally + only dispute where administering state has built civilian residential infrastructure to entrench sovereignty + 70+ year continuous Korean Coast Guard presence framework.
PA-recognition: silent statutory + no-apex-position + silent regulator.

Joins Sea-of-Japan/East-Sea + civil-law + South-Korean-administered-disputed-territory cluster + US-neutrality-between-democratic-allies-globally-distinctive + sovereignty-entrenchment-civilian-residence + purely-bilateral-Korea-Japan-dispute + Syngman-Rhee-Line + Korean-Hague-2012-accession-with-Japanese-non-recognition clusters within the corpus.
